MS in Business Analytics, UC San Diego
I am a graduate student in Business Analytics at UC San Diego with interests in data analysis, business strategy, and decision-making through data-driven insights. This website presents my academic background, selected projects, and coursework completed for MGTA 495.

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
feels static
less visual interest
looks like plain notes






draws attention
feels modern
more polished





Even very small motion can make a Quarto site feel more intentional and more professional.

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
Three easy animation ideas




Level 1


Fade-in entrance
Content appears softly when the slide step is revealed.

opacity + translateY + keyframes






Level 2


Hover lift
A card slightly rises and gains shadow when the mouse moves over it.

transition + transform + box-shadow






Level 3


Button micro-interaction
Small motion on buttons makes your page feel more interactive.

Click me






These three are a good starting point because they are easy to build, easy to control, and already make a website look much more polished.

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
How these animations actually work

The basic logic


1. Add a class to the element
In HTML, a class is just a label you attach to an element so CSS can style it.
HTML: <div class="hover-lift">This card lifts on hover</div>
Quarto: ::: {.hover-lift} This card lifts on hover :::
In other words, Quarto’s ::: block often plays the same role as an HTML <div>.


2. Define the style in CSS
Then CSS tells that class what to do. A simplified example：
.hover-lift { transition: transform 0.3s ease; }
.hover-lift:hover { transform: translateY(-8px); }


3. Trigger the animation
For entrance effects, RevealJS can reveal a fragment step by step. For hover effects, the trigger is simply :hover.

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
What else is possible?
These are just examples, not the only options.



Fancy 01


Glassmorphism cards
Soft transparency, blur, layered shadows, and subtle floating motion.




Fancy 02


Shimmer loading effect
A moving light sweep adds energy to cards, sections, or callouts.




Fancy 03


Glow borders
Animated gradient borders create a more premium, high-tech feel.




Fancy 04


Orbital highlight
A blurred glow rotates around the card to create a dynamic spotlight effect.




Fancy 05


Gradient text + animated underline
Great for hero titles, featured sections, or portfolio headers.




Fancy 06


Staggered entrance system
Multiple items appear one after another for a more designed presentation flow.

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
Where to find inspiration & resources




Inspiration

Design inspiration
Dribbble → animation + UI ideas
Awwwards → high-end web interactions
Behance → full project case studies
Pinterest → quick visual exploration

Search keywords: “UI animation”, “micro interaction”





Tools & Code

Animation resources
CodePen → ready-to-use CSS demos
GSAP → advanced animation library
Animate.css → simple plug-and-play effects
UI libraries (Tailwind UI, Flowbite)

Copy → tweak → integrate into Quarto





Good animation design is rarely invented from scratch. Most of the time, you observe → copy → simplify → adapt.


Final tip

Think like a designer, not just a coder
Don’t add animation everywhere, use it in an appropriate way

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text:
In the interest of fostering an open and welcoming environment, we as contributors and maintainers pledge to making participation in our project and our community a harassment-free experience for everyone, regardless of age, body size, disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity and expression, level of experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, religion, or sexual identity and orientation.



Examples of behavior that contributes to creating a positive environment include:

Using welcoming and inclusive language
Being respectful of differing viewpoints and experiences
Gracefully accepting constructive criticism
Focusing on what is best for the community
Showing empathy towards other community members

Examples of unacceptable behavior by participants include:

The use of sexualized language or imagery and unwelcome sexual attention or advances
Trolling, insulting/derogatory comments, and personal or political attacks
Public or private harassment
Publishing others’ private information, such as a physical or electronic address, without explicit permission
Other conduct which could reasonably be considered inappropriate in a professional setting




Project maintainers are responsible for clarifying the standards of acceptable behavior and are expected to take appropriate and fair corrective action in response to any instances of unacceptable behavior.
Project maintainers have the right and responsibility to remove, edit, or reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, and other contributions that are not aligned to this Code of Conduct, or to ban temporarily or permanently any contributor for other behaviors that they deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, or harmful.



This Code of Conduct applies both within project spaces and in public spaces when an individual is representing the project or its community. Examples of representing a project or community include using an official project e-mail address, posting via an official social media account, or acting as an appointed representative at an online or offline event. Representation of a project may be further defined and clarified by project maintainers.



Instances of abusive, harassing, or otherwise unacceptable behavior may be reported by contacting the project team at vbhogayata@gmail.com. All complaints will be reviewed and investigated and will result in a response that is deemed necessary and appropriate to the circumstances. The project team is obligated to maintain confidentiality with regard to the reporter of an incident. Further details of specific enforcement policies may be posted separately.
Project maintainers who do not follow or enforce the Code of Conduct in good faith may face temporary or permanent repercussions as determined by other members of the project’s leadership.



This Code of Conduct is adapted from the Contributor Covenant, version 1.4, available at https://www.contributor-covenant.org/version/1/4/code-of-conduct.html
For answers to common questions about this code of conduct, see https://www.contributor-covenant.org/faq

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
Why animation matters


Without animation

feels static
less visual interest
looks like plain notes


With animation

draws attention
feels modern
more polished



Even very small motion can make a Quarto site feel more intentional and more professional.

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
Three easy animation ideas




Level 1

Fade-in entrance
Content appears softly when the slide step is revealed.

opacity + translateY + keyframes





Level 2

Hover lift
A card slightly rises and gains shadow when the mouse moves over it.

transition + transform + box-shadow





Level 3

Button micro-interaction
Small motion on buttons makes your page feel more interactive.

Click me




These three are a good starting point because they are easy to build, easy to control, and already make a website look much more polished.

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
How these animations actually work
The basic logic

1. Add a class to the element
In HTML, a class is just a label you attach to an element so CSS can style it.
HTML: <div class="hover-lift">This card lifts on hover</div>
Quarto: ::: {.hover-lift} This card lifts on hover :::
In other words, Quarto’s ::: block often plays the same role as an HTML <div>.


2. Define the style in CSS
Then CSS tells that class what to do. A simplified example:
.hover-lift { transition: transform 0.3s ease; }
.hover-lift:hover { transform: translateY(-8px); }


3. Trigger the animation
For entrance effects, RevealJS can reveal a fragment step by step. For hover effects, the trigger is simply :hover.

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website

text:
Where to find inspiration & resources




Inspiration

Design inspiration
Dribbble → animation + UI ideas
Awwwards → high-end web interactions
Behance → full project case studies
Pinterest → quick visual exploration

Search keywords: “UI animation”, “micro interaction”





Tools & Code

Animation resources
CodePen → ready-to-use CSS demos
GSAP → advanced animation library
Animate.css → simple plug-and-play effects
UI libraries (Tailwind UI, Flowbite)

Copy → tweak → integrate into Quarto




Good animation design is rarely invented from scratch. Most of the time, you observe → copy → simplify → adapt.

Final tip

Think like a designer, not just a coder
Don’t add animation everywhere, use it in an appropriate way

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
How these animations actually work
The basic logic

1. Add a class to the element
In HTML, a class is just a label you attach to an element so CSS can style it.
HTML: <div class="hover-lift">This card lifts on hover</div>
Quarto: ::: {.hover-lift} This card lifts on hover :::
In other words, Quarto’s ::: block often plays the same role as an HTML div.


2. Define the style in CSS
Then CSS tells that class what to do. A simplified example:
.hover-lift { transition: transform 0.3s ease; }
.hover-lift:hover { transform: translateY(-8px); }


3. Trigger the animation
For entrance effects, RevealJS can reveal a fragment step by step. For hover effects, the trigger is simply :hover.


4. Use animation or transition
animation is good for entrance effects. transition is good for hover and button interactions.

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
What else is possible?
These are just examples, not the only options.



Fancy 01

Glassmorphism cards
Soft transparency, blur, layered shadows, and subtle floating motion.



Fancy 02

Shimmer loading effect
A moving light sweep adds energy to cards, sections, or callouts.



Fancy 03

Glow borders
Animated gradient borders create a more premium, high-tech feel.



Fancy 04

Orbital highlight
A blurred glow rotates around the card to create a dynamic spotlight effect.



Fancy 05

Gradient text + animated underline
Great for hero titles, featured sections, or portfolio headers.



Fancy 06

Staggered entrance system
Multiple items appear one after another for a more designed presentation flow.

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
Takeaway

Start with one entrance animation such as fade-in
Add one interaction pattern such as hover lift
Improve buttons with micro-interactions
Use fancier effects only where you want emphasis

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
Where to find inspiration
If you want to use animation to make a website look better, there are many good places to find ideas and examples.



Resource 01

CodePen
A huge collection of live animation demos. You can search for ideas like CSS animation, hover effects, cards, buttons, or page transitions.



Resource 02

Awwwards
A gallery of high-end website design. This is useful for design inspiration and for seeing how motion is used in real websites.



Resource 03

Dribbble
A good place to find modern UI ideas, micro-interactions, and polished visual styles.



Resource 04

GitHub and public Quarto examples
You can look at public Quarto websites and templates to borrow layout ideas, animation ideas, and CSS structure.



Resource 05

ChatGPT or other AI tools
AI can help generate CSS and animation ideas quickly, but it is still important to read the code and understand it.



Resource 06

CSS-Tricks
A useful site for learning how CSS animations and transitions actually work.



A good workflow is: find inspiration, simplify the idea, and adapt it to your own website style. You do not need very complex effects — even small motion can make a page feel more polished.

title: Coursework
text:
Relevant Coursework

A/B Testing (MGTA 495)
Simulation of A/B experiments, hypothesis testing, and statistical inference.
Key Topics - Experimental design

Hypothesis testing
Bootstrap standard errors
Central Limit Theorem
A/B test evaluation

title: Coursework
text:
Academic Interests
My current interests include: - business intelligence, - strategic analysis, - customer and market insights, - and practical applications of analytics in organizations.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Note



Write a short introduction for the reader that sets the scene. Cover the following points in your own words:

You have a website and your landing page includes a place where visitors can sign up to receive your newsletter.
You want to test which of two possible “calls to action” (CTAs) leads to more sign-ups. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (you are welcome to change the language of the CTAs).
You will evaluate which CTA performs better by running an A/B test. Explain what an A/B test is: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and you compare sign-up rates between the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Note



Write a short introduction for the reader that sets the scene. Cover the following points in your own words:

You have a website and your landing page includes a place where visitors can sign up to receive your newsletter.
You want to test which of two possible “calls to action” (CTAs) leads to more sign-ups. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (you are welcome to change the language of the CTAs).
You will evaluate which CTA performs better by running an A/B test. Explain what an A/B test is: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and you compare sign-up rates between the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem






Note



In this section, connect the A/B test to a formal statistical framework. Address each of the following ideas:

Each visitor either signs up or does not, so we can model each visitor’s outcome as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution with parameter \(\pi\), the probability of signing up.
Because the CTA a visitor sees may influence their probability of signing up, we allow the parameter to differ across groups: visitors who see CTA A have sign-up probability \(\pi_A\), and visitors who see CTA B have sign-up probability \(\pi_B\). Each visitor can either sign up (1) or not (0).
The quantity we care about is \(\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B\), the difference in sign-up rates between the two CTAs.
Our estimator of \(\theta\) is the difference in sample means, \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\). (Since each observation is either 0 or 1, the sample mean is simply the proportion of visitors who signed up — so \(\bar{X}_A = \hat\pi_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat\pi_B\).)

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data






Note




Write a brief paragraph for the reader explaining that in the real world we would not know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\), but for the purpose of this exercise we will set them ourselves so that we can study the behavior of our estimator when we know the truth.
Suppose \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and \(\pi_B = 0.18\), so the true difference is \(\theta = 0.04\).
Using a language of your choice, simulate 1,000 draws from each of the two Bernoulli distributions (2,000 total). Store the results so you can use them in the sections that follow.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers






Note




Write an introductory sentence or two explaining the Law of Large Numbers to the reader: as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. Connect this back to our estimator — the LLN is what gives us confidence that \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) will be close to the true \(\theta\) when our sample is large enough.
Then demonstrate this visually. Using the simulated data from the previous section, compute the element-wise differences between the CTA A draws and the CTA B draws to get a vector of 1,000 differences. Calculate the cumulative average of that vector (i.e., the running mean after 1 draw, after 2 draws, …, after 1,000 draws) and plot it against the number of observations.
The plot should show a noisy average when only a few observations are included that settles down and converges toward the true difference of 0.04 as the sample size grows.
After the plot, write a few sentences explaining what the reader should take away from it.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors






Note




Now that we “trust” our estimator, the next natural question is: how precise is it? We would like to report not just a point estimate of \(\theta\) but also a confidence interval that communicates our uncertainty.
Write a short introduction explaining the idea of bootstrapping to the reader. The bootstrap is a resampling method that lets us estimate the variability of a statistic without relying on a closed-form formula. We resample (with replacement) from our observed data many times, compute the statistic of interest on each resample, and use the standard deviation of those resampled statistics as our estimate of the standard error.
Then carry out the following steps:

From your 1,000 CTA A draws and 1,000 CTA B draws, draw a bootstrap resample of size 1,000 (with replacement) from each group. Compute \(\hat\theta^* = \bar{X}_A^* - \bar{X}_B^*\) for that resample.
Repeat step 1 a total of 1,000 times to produce a vector of 1,000 bootstrapped estimates of \(\theta\).
Compute the standard deviation of those 1,000 values. This is your bootstrapped standard error.
Compare it to the analytical standard error. For the difference in means of two independent Bernoulli samples, the standard error formula is:


\[
SE(\hat\theta) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat\pi_A(1 - \hat\pi_A)}{n_A} + \frac{\hat\pi_B(1 - \hat\pi_B)}{n_B}}
\]
where \(\hat\pi_A\) and \(\hat\pi_B\) are the sample proportions and \(n_A\) and \(n_B\) are the sample sizes.

Report both the bootstrapped standard error and the analytical standard error and comment on how close they are to each other. Then use the bootstrapped standard error to construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\theta\) and interpret it for the reader.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem






Note




Write a short introduction explaining the Central Limit Theorem to the reader: regardless of the shape of the underlying population distribution, the sampling distribution of the sample mean (and therefore of the difference in sample means) becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows.
Then demonstrate this visually by creating four histograms, one for each of the following sample sizes: \(n \in \{25, 50, 100, 500\}\).
For each sample size \(n\):

Draw \(n\) observations from the CTA A distribution and \(n\) observations from the CTA B distribution.
Compute \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).
Repeat steps 1–2 a total of 1,000 times to produce 1,000 values of \(\hat\theta\).
Plot a histogram of those 1,000 values.

Arrange the four histograms so they can be compared easily (e.g., in a 2x2 grid with consistent x-axis scales).
After the plots, write a few sentences explaining what the reader should observe: at small sample sizes the histogram may look rough or asymmetric, but as \(n\) increases the distribution of \(\hat\theta\) becomes smoother and more bell-shaped, illustrating the Central Limit Theorem in action.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing






Note




Now that we have seen the CLT in action — that the sampling distribution of \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples — we can use this result to perform a formal hypothesis test.
Write a paragraph explaining what a hypothesis test is to the reader. We set up a null hypothesis \(H_0: \theta = 0\) (i.e., the two CTAs produce the same sign-up rate) and an alternative hypothesis \(H_1: \theta \neq 0\). The goal is to assess whether the data provide enough evidence to reject \(H_0\).
Then explain why we standardize \(\hat\theta\) to form a test statistic. We compute:

\[
z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}
\]

Walk the reader through the logic of this standardization step by step:

By the CLT, we know that \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples. Under \(H_0\), the mean of that distribution is 0.
When we divide a Normal random variable by its standard deviation, we get a standard Normal \(N(0,1)\). So under \(H_0\), \(z \;\dot\sim\; N(0,1)\).
This means we know the distribution of our test statistic under the null, which is exactly what we need to compute a p-value.

Explain to the reader why this is sometimes called a “t-test” even though the test statistic follows an approximate Normal distribution, not a t-distribution. The classical t-test arises when the data are drawn from a Normal distribution and the variance must be estimated — the ratio then follows an exact t-distribution. In our setting the data are Bernoulli (not Normal), so there is no exact t-distribution result. The CLT gives us approximate Normality, and since we estimate the SE from the data, what we are really doing is closer to a z-test. In practice, for large samples the t-distribution and the standard Normal are nearly identical, so the distinction has little practical consequence — but it is important to understand why.
Using your simulated data, compute the test statistic and its associated p-value (using the standard Normal distribution as the reference). Interpret the result for the reader.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression






Note




It turns out that the two-sample test we just ran is mathematically equivalent to a simple linear regression. Understanding this connection is useful because it means the same machinery — and the same software — that you use for regression can also perform hypothesis tests, and it generalizes naturally to more complex experimental designs.
Explain the setup to the reader. Stack the CTA A and CTA B data into a single dataset with two columns: an outcome \(Y_i\) (1 if the visitor signed up, 0 otherwise) and an indicator \(D_i\) (1 if the visitor saw CTA A, 0 if they saw CTA B). Then fit the regression:

\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i
\]

Walk through the interpretation of the coefficients:

\(\beta_0\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 0\) — i.e., the mean of the B group, which estimates \(\pi_B\).
\(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 1\) — i.e., the mean of the A group, which estimates \(\pi_A\).
Therefore \(\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\), and the OLS estimate \(\hat\beta_1\) is numerically identical to \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).

Fit this regression using your simulated data. Report the coefficient estimate \(\hat\beta_1\), its standard error, its t-statistic, and its p-value. Compare these to the values you computed in the previous section using the two-sample approach — they should match (up to a small difference in how the standard error is computed; standard OLS assumes a single pooled variance, while the two-sample formula we used assumes separate variances for each group).
Explain to the reader why this equivalence matters: the regression framework makes it easy to add controls, interactions, or additional treatment arms, all while producing the same inference in the simple case.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking






Note




Your boss is impatient. Rather than waiting until all 1,000 visitors have been assigned to each group, she wants to run the hypothesis test after every 100 visitors per group — peeking at the results after 100, 200, 300, …, up to 1,000 visitors per group (10 tests in total). If any of those tests comes back significant at the \(\alpha = 0.05\) level, she wants to declare a winner and stop the experiment early.
Explain to the reader why this is problematic under classical frequentist statistics. The key issue is that a single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% chance of a false positive (rejecting \(H_0\) when it is actually true). But when you conduct multiple tests on accumulating data, each peek is another opportunity to get a false positive. The overall probability of at least one false rejection across all the peeks is much higher than 5%.
Demonstrate this with a simulation. The idea is as follows:

Set up a world where the null hypothesis is true: \(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\) (i.e., \(\theta = 0\), there is no difference between the CTAs).
Simulate a single experiment: draw 1,000 observations for group A and 1,000 for group B. After every 100 observations per group (at \(n = 100, 200, \ldots, 1000\)), compute the z-statistic and p-value. Record whether any of the 10 p-values falls below 0.05.
Repeat step 2 a total of 10,000 times to simulate 10,000 experiments.
Compute the fraction of those 10,000 experiments in which at least one peek produced a significant result. This is the empirical false positive rate under peeking.

Compare this to the nominal 5% false positive rate of a single test. Report the result and explain to the reader why peeking inflates the Type I error rate and what this means in practice for running A/B tests.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
A landing page usually asks for only a tiny action from a visitor: one click, one email address, one moment of attention. Yet small wording changes can make that request feel either inviting or easy to ignore. That is exactly why call-to-action copy is such a natural setting for A/B testing.
Imagine a homepage with a newsletter signup box. One version of the page uses the line CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!” A second version uses CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!” The business question is simple: which message leads to more subscriptions?
An A/B test answers that question by splitting incoming traffic at random. Some visitors see version A and others see version B. Once enough people have passed through the page, the two sign-up rates can be compared. Behind that very practical workflow sits a deeper statistical story about estimation, uncertainty, sampling variation, and false positives. The goal of this post is to use one simple experiment to walk through those core ideas in classical frequentist inference.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
A landing page usually asks for only a tiny action from a visitor: one click, one email address, one moment of attention. Yet small wording changes can make that request feel either inviting or easy to ignore. That is exactly why call-to-action copy is such a natural setting for A/B testing.
Imagine a homepage with a newsletter signup box. One version of the page uses the line CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!” A second version uses CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!” The business question is simple: which message leads to more subscriptions?
An A/B test answers that question by splitting incoming traffic at random. Some visitors see version A and others see version B. Once enough people have passed through the page, the two sign-up rates can be compared. Behind that very practical workflow sits a deeper statistical story about estimation, uncertainty, sampling variation, and false positives. The goal of this post is to use one simple experiment to walk through those core ideas in classical frequentist inference.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
At the individual level, each visitor generates a binary outcome: they either subscribe or they do not. That makes the Bernoulli model a natural starting point. If \(\pi\) denotes the probability that a visitor signs up, then each observation is a draw from a Bernoulli distribution with parameter \(\pi\).
The key complication is that the probability of signing up may depend on which CTA the visitor sees. Let \(\pi_A\) denote the sign-up probability under CTA A and let \(\pi_B\) denote the sign-up probability under CTA B. The quantity of real interest is not either probability by itself, but their difference:
\[
\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B
\]
If \(\theta\) is positive, CTA A performs better. If it is negative, CTA B performs better. If it is zero, the two messages are equally effective.
In practice, the true value of \(\theta\) is unknown and must be estimated from data. A natural estimator is the difference in sample means:
\[
\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B
\]
Because each outcome is either 0 or 1, the sample mean in each group is just the observed sign-up rate. So \(\bar{X}_A = \hat{\pi}_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat{\pi}_B\). In other words, the estimator here is nothing more exotic than the observed difference in conversion rates across the two page variants.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In a real experiment, the truth sits behind the data and is never directly visible. Simulation turns that relationship around: the truth is specified first, and the observed data are generated from it. That makes simulation a useful way to study how a statistical method behaves when the answer is already known.
For the rest of this post, suppose CTA A has a true sign-up probability of \(0.22\) and CTA B has a true sign-up probability of \(0.18\). Under those assumptions, the true treatment effect is
\[
\theta = 0.22 - 0.18 = 0.04
\]
So the “better” CTA improves sign-up rates by four percentage points. The analysis below begins by simulating 1,000 visitors for each group, creating a synthetic experiment with 2,000 total observations.








Simulated A/B Test Data


1,000 visitors per CTA


Group
True sign-up probability
Simulated sample proportion
Sample size




CTA A
22.0%
21.1%
1000


CTA B
18.0%
17.2%
1000







As expected, the simulated sample proportions are close to the truth but not identical to it. That small gap is not a mistake; it is the whole point. Even when the underlying probabilities are fixed, random samples still wobble.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
One reason analysts trust sample averages at all is the Law of Large Numbers. Informally, it says that averages become more stable as more observations accumulate. In the A/B setting, that idea explains why the difference in observed sign-up rates eventually becomes a reliable guide to the true treatment effect.
To make that visible, the two simulated outcome vectors can be differenced observation by observation, and the running average of those differences can be tracked as the sample grows from 1 pair to 1,000 pairs.









The beginning of the curve is chaotic. With only a handful of observations, one extra signup or one extra non-signup can move the average sharply. But that instability fades as the sample grows. The curve does not become perfectly flat, yet it clearly settles toward the true effect. This is the first major lesson of the post: large samples do not eliminate randomness, but they do discipline it.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
A point estimate answers only one question: what effect seems most plausible given the observed data? It does not answer the equally important question of how noisy that estimate is. Two experiments may produce the same estimated treatment effect but differ dramatically in how uncertain that estimate should feel.
The bootstrap offers a practical way to measure that uncertainty. Rather than relying entirely on a closed-form formula, the bootstrap repeatedly resamples from the observed data, computes the statistic of interest in each resample, and then uses the spread of those resampled statistics as an estimate of the standard error.








Bootstrap Results


Uncertainty around the treatment effect


Statistic
Value




Estimated treatment effect
3.90%


Bootstrap standard error
1.71%


Analytical standard error
1.76%


95% CI lower bound
0.55%


95% CI upper bound
7.25%
















Two things stand out here. First, the bootstrap standard error and the analytical standard error are very close, which is a reassuring sign that the uncertainty estimate is not an artifact of one particular method. Second, the resulting 95% confidence interval excludes zero. In plain language, the observed data are not just consistent with CTA A being better; they are inconsistent with the idea that the two messages perform equally well.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The next step is to move from one experiment to the behavior of the estimator across many hypothetical repetitions of the experiment. This is where the Central Limit Theorem becomes useful. Even when the underlying data are Bernoulli rather than Normally distributed, the sampling distribution of a sample mean becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows. Because \(\hat{\theta}\) is built from sample means, the same logic applies to it as well.
To show that visually, the simulation below repeats the entire A/B test 1,000 times for each of four sample sizes: \(n \in \{25, 50, 100, 500\}\).









The smaller-sample histograms are rough and uneven, which makes sense: with limited data, there is still a lot of discreteness and noise in the estimator. But as the sample size increases, the histograms become smoother, tighter, and more bell-shaped. That is the CLT at work. It is also the bridge from descriptive simulation to formal inference, because approximate Normality is what makes z-based testing possible in large samples.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
At this point the analysis has everything needed for a formal test. The business question becomes a statistical one: could an observed difference of this size reasonably have appeared if the two CTAs were actually identical?
The null and alternative hypotheses are
\[
H_0: \theta = 0
\]
and
\[
H_1: \theta \neq 0
\]
respectively.
The observed estimate is converted into a test statistic by scaling it relative to its own uncertainty:
\[
z = \frac{\hat{\theta} - 0}{SE(\hat{\theta})}
\]
That standardization matters. Under the CLT, \(\hat{\theta}\) is approximately Normal in large samples, and under the null its mean is zero. Once divided by its standard error, the statistic is approximately distributed as \(N(0,1)\). That reference distribution is what makes a p-value possible.
Strictly speaking, this setup is closer to a large-sample z-test than to a classical t-test. The exact t framework belongs to Normal data with estimated variance. Here the data are Bernoulli, so the Normal approximation comes from asymptotics rather than an exact finite-sample theorem. In a sample this large, the distinction is practically minor, but conceptually it is worth keeping straight.








Hypothesis Test for the Difference in Sign-up Rates


Large-sample z-based inference


Quantity
Value




Estimated effect
3.90%


Standard error
1.76%


z-statistic
2.22


Two-sided p-value
0.0265







The p-value is small enough to reject the null at conventional significance levels. In practical terms, the observed gap between the two CTAs is too large to dismiss as ordinary sampling noise. The data point toward a real performance difference.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
One of the most elegant parts of this example is that the two-sample test can be rewritten as a regression with almost no extra machinery. That matters because regression is the language most empirical work eventually speaks.
Stack the two groups into one dataset. Let \(Y_i\) equal 1 if visitor \(i\) signs up and 0 otherwise, and let \(D_i\) equal 1 if that visitor saw CTA A and 0 if they saw CTA B. Then estimate
\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i
\]
In this model, \(\beta_0\) is the mean outcome for the B group, so it estimates \(\pi_B\). The sum \(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the mean outcome for the A group, so it estimates \(\pi_A\). That means \(\beta_1\) itself is exactly the treatment effect:
\[
\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta
\]
So the regression coefficient on the treatment indicator should match the earlier difference-in-means estimate.








Two-Sample Test and Regression Comparison


Same estimate, nearly identical inference


Method
Estimate
Std. Error
Statistic
p-value




Two-sample approach
3.90%
1.76%
2.22
0.0265


Regression approach
3.90%
1.76%
2.22
0.0267







The numbers line up almost perfectly. That is not a coincidence; it is the same comparison written in two dialects. The practical value of the regression version is that it scales gracefully. Once the problem is in regression form, adding covariates, interactions, or multiple treatments becomes straightforward.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
So far, the story has assumed a well-behaved experiment: run the test, collect the planned sample, analyze once. Real organizations are rarely that patient. A manager may want to peek at results every few hours and stop the test the moment one variant looks significant.
That instinct is understandable but statistically dangerous. A single test at the 5% significance level has a 5% chance of generating a false positive when the null is true. Repeating the test over and over on accumulating data creates multiple chances to get lucky noise. The overall false-positive rate can climb far above 5%.
To see how large that distortion can be, the simulation below creates a world where there is no true difference at all: \(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\). Each experiment is checked after every 100 observations per group, up to 1,000 observations per group, and the process records whether any interim look crosses the 0.05 threshold.








Peeking Inflates Type I Error


Sequential looks create extra false-positive opportunities


Scenario
False positive rate




Single test at alpha = 0.05
5.0%


Peeking every 100 visitors per group
19.3%
















The result is sobering. A process that feels like “just checking in” can nearly quadruple the false-positive rate. For A/B testing in practice, that means patience is not merely a managerial virtue; it is part of valid inference. If early stopping is going to be allowed, it needs a method designed for sequential testing rather than ordinary repeated p-values.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Conclusion
A/B testing often looks like a lightweight product tool, but under the surface it is a compact lesson in frequentist statistics. The difference in observed conversion rates is an estimator; the Law of Large Numbers explains why it stabilizes; the bootstrap shows how uncertainty can be quantified; the Central Limit Theorem explains why Normal-based inference becomes reasonable; and hypothesis testing turns that logic into a formal decision rule.
The regression reframing shows that this same experiment fits naturally into a broader modeling framework, while the peeking example shows how easy it is to misuse statistical evidence when operational convenience takes over. That, in the end, may be the most useful lesson from the entire exercise: the strength of an A/B test comes not only from having data, but from respecting the statistical logic that makes those data trustworthy.
